title: JavaScript Concepts
response:
Type coercion is JavaScript's automatic (or explicit) conversion of values from one type to another.

Implicit coercion (JS decides) — dangerous if unaware:
• + with a string converts the other operand to string
• ==  (loose equality) coerces before comparing
• Truthy/falsy rules govern if, &&, ||, !

Explicit coercion (you decide) — safe and clear:
• Number(), parseInt(), parseFloat() — to number
• String() or .toString() — to string
• Boolean() — to boolean

Always prefer === (strict equality) to avoid coercion surprises.
code_example:
// Implicit — surprising results
console.log(1 + '2');      // '12'  (number → string)
console.log('5' - 2);      // 3     (string → number)
console.log('' == false);  // true  (loose equality)
console.log(0 == false);   // true

// Explicit — predictable
console.log(Number('42')); // 42
console.log(String(99));   // '99'
console.log(Boolean(0));   // false
console.log(Boolean(''));  // false

// Always use strict equality
console.log(1 === '1');    // false ✅
